era_context: The late 14th century: Ming China under the Hongwu Emperor; Ottoman expansion in the Balkans; Western European kingdoms in dynastic conflict; the Great Schism fragmenting papal authority; the Timurid Empire in Central Asia approaching its succession crisis.

events.title: Zheng He born (traditional date); future Ming admiral
events.description: Zheng He, the famous Ming admiral and explorer, was born in 1371 according to most sources, though some traditions place his birth in 1393. He was a Muslim eunuch of Mongol descent who would later command the massive treasure fleets (1405–1433) that explored the Indian Ocean, East Africa, and beyond. His voyages represented the peak of Ming maritime power before the dynasty turned inward. The uncertainty in his birth date reflects the difficulty of dating events in the lives of eunuchs, who were often not recorded in standard genealogies.

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming)

events.certainty_note: Zheng He's birth date is uncertain. Most scholars accept 1371, but some sources cite 1393. The discrepancy may reflect confusion between his birth and his entry into service. Included here as a historiographic artifact of dating uncertainty.

events.title: Gian Galeazzo Visconti consolidates power in Milan
events.description: Gian Galeazzo Visconti, the Duke of Milan, was consolidating his power and expanding Milanese territory in the 1390s. In 1393, he was engaged in diplomatic and military maneuvering to extend Visconti influence across northern Italy. He would eventually become one of the most powerful rulers in Italy, creating a regional state that rivaled Venice and Florence. His patronage of the arts and architecture (including the Milan Cathedral, begun 1386) made Milan a center of Renaissance culture. He died in 1402, and his state fragmented.
key_figures: Gian Galeazzo Visconti (Duke of Milan, r. 1378–1402)
events.certainty_note: Gian Galeazzo's consolidation of power occurred over the 1380s–1390s; 1393 is an approximate date for his peak influence.

events.title: Council of Constance preparations; Great Schism unresolved
events.description: The Great Schism (1378–1417) remained unresolved in 1393, with rival popes in Rome and Avignon claiming legitimacy. The Church was fragmented, and efforts to resolve the schism through councils and diplomatic pressure were ongoing. The Council of Constance (1414–1418) would eventually resolve the schism, but in 1393, the crisis was still acute. This period saw intense theological debate, political maneuvering by European monarchs, and the rise of reform movements (e.g., Jan Hus) that would challenge papal authority.
events.certainty_note: The Great Schism and its dates are well-documented in papal and ecclesiastical records.

events.title: Bayezid I consolidates Ottoman power after Murad I's death
events.description: Bayezid I (r. 1389–1402), known as 'Yildirim' (the Thunderbolt), had succeeded his father Murad I in 1389 and was consolidating Ottoman power in the early 1390s. By 1393, he had secured his position against internal rivals and was expanding Ottoman territory in Anatolia and the Balkans. His reign saw rapid military expansion and the transformation of the Ottoman state into a major regional power. He would eventually face Timur in 1402 at the Battle of Ankara, a catastrophic defeat that temporarily halted Ottoman expansion.
events.certainty_note: Bayezid I's reign and consolidation of power are well-documented in Ottoman and Byzantine sources.

events.title: Clement VII (Avignon Pope) dies; Benedict XIII succeeds
events.description: Pope Clement VII of the Avignon line died on 16 September 1394 (not 1393; see disconfirming evidence). However, 1393 marks the final year of his papacy and the anticipation of succession. His death would be followed by the election of Benedict XIII (Pedro de Luna), who would continue the Avignon papacy and prolong the Great Schism. This succession was a critical moment in the schism, as it represented the Avignon cardinals' commitment to maintaining their rival papacy.
events.certainty_note: Clement VII died in September 1394, not 1393. This entry marks 1393 as the final year of his papacy.

disconfirming_evidence: Clement VII's death is dated to September 1394, not 1393. Zheng He's birth date is uncertain; most sources place it in 1371, not 1393. The entry above has been corrected to reflect these uncertainties. Gian Galeazzo Visconti's consolidation of power was a gradual process spanning the 1380s–1390s, not a discrete event in 1393.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 1393 is dominated by European and Islamic written sources. Chinese sources are abundant but focus on court politics. Sub-Saharan African, Pacific, and interior American events are almost entirely absent. The sources are predominantly later compilations rather than contemporary records, introducing dating uncertainty and potential bias toward court-sponsored narratives.

graph_edges.note: Zheng He's birth (or entry into service) preceded his famous voyages
The unresolved Great Schism of 1393 led to the Council of Constance in 1414
Bayezid I's consolidation of Ottoman power led to his confrontation with Timur in 1402
Clement VII's final year coincided with the Great Schism crisis
